title: #architecture: 1739450020
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off a thread to coordinate the architecture review for route-level quantization enablement + fallback behavior. Need sign-off this week to keep the schema/API/routing work unblocked.

Talia Benson: ADR draft is here (WIP): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0137-Route-level-quantization-profile-enablement

Talia Benson: Proposed review slot: Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. Attendees: Platform (Priya/Ethan/Mina/Ravi/Jordan), Runtime (Mateo/Noah), Console (Dylan/Chloe), Security/RBAC (Naomi/Marcus), PM (Marissa). If you can’t make it, pls comment on the ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. Main thing from Product side: must be explicitly opt-in per route for v1, no global “tenant default enables everything.” Also needs “easy rollback” story that Support can execute.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks Talia. Read through ADR quickly. Overall direction seems right. I want to make sure we’re crisp on:
- what exactly is stored in route config (profile_id vs profile_ref vs alias)
- where compatibility is enforced (policy engine vs runtime)
- deterministic resolution (same request config => same profile)

Ethan Park: I can make Thu.
Question: ADR says “route references a profile ref string” and examples show both `recommended` and a versioned id. We should avoid anything that can change under customer feet.

Mina Haddad: +1. If we allow `recommended` alias, then “recommended” moving to a new version changes behavior without an explicit config write. That’s basically hidden rollout.

Ravi Menon: Agree. My suggestion: v1 should only allow explicit versioned `quant_profile_id` (immutable). If we need an alias later, treat it as a separate field with “pinning” semantics.

Jordan Lee: Determinism hat on: any alias that’s resolved at request time based on “latest” breaks determinism unless we snapshot it into resolved policy at write time.

Noah Patel: Runtime side: we can handle either, but if policy engine resolves to an explicit immutable id before the request hits the runtime, it’s cleaner. Runtime should never call “what’s the newest profile” mid-request.

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Runtime should consume resolved result: {enabled, profile_id, reason_if_fallback}. Everything else belongs in control plane.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX question: customers will ask “just use recommended.” But we can still show “recommended” in UI and when they click enable, we write the pinned versioned ID. That gives UX simplicity without the hidden change risk.

Chloe Martin: That’s workable for backend too. Console can call a “preview” endpoint that returns which profile id would be pinned + compatibility warnings.

Naomi Feldman: For audit/compliance: we need the audit event to include the exact pinned profile id and diff. “Enabled recommended” is not sufficient for change control.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also need clarity on who can enable/disable this. Ideally a separate permission (route config write but with elevated capability). At minimum, record actor + route + old/new profile.

Talia Benson: Sounds like we’re converging on: UI may say “recommended” but route config stores immutable `quant_profile_id` (or `default`). I’ll add that as a decision point in ADR.

Priya Natarajan: On compatibility enforcement: ADR currently says policy engine checks model/hardware/seq length and falls back to baseline. Runtime also has guardrails.
We should decide: which layer is authoritative? (I’d like policy engine to be authoritative; runtime should still validate to protect itself.)

Jordan Lee: Agree. Policy eval should produce a single resolved decision, including fallback, to avoid oscillation.
Also: if runtime independently decides to fallback, we must avoid a feedback loop where policy keeps trying and runtime keeps rejecting.

Ravi Menon: +1 on “no oscillation.” I propose we include a `resolution_outcome` in the resolved policy: `APPLIED | FALLBACK_INCOMPATIBLE | DISABLED | KILL_SWITCH`.
Runtime logs it but shouldn’t re-resolve.

Noah Patel: Runtime still has to protect against mismatched artifacts / corrupted profile / unsupported kernel path. In those cases we should fail closed to baseline and emit metrics.
But we should NOT attempt to re-query policy. Just do local fallback.

Mateo Alvarez: Exactly. Runtime local fallback is last resort; also needs a counter so SRE can alert when it happens.

Connor O'Brien: From release/oncall POV: we need a clear kill switch behavior in the ADR:
- global kill switch disables application everywhere (even if route is enabled)
- should be fast propagating (minutes)
- should still keep system healthy (fallback baseline)

Mina Haddad: Routing view: if kill switch is on, route selection should still pick the same model variant; only precision/quant mode changes.
Do not redirect to different region/tier unless we’re already in a failure mode.

Ethan Park: Another detail: caching.
- route config service caching resolved settings is good
- but we must ensure config updates invalidate cache quickly
- and resolved decision is included in request context so runtime doesn’t do extra calls

Chloe Martin: +1. Our control plane endpoint can return “resolved quant settings” with a TTL + etag. Router can cache on route_id + model_variant + region.

Priya Natarajan: Fallback semantics: ADR says “fallback to baseline precision.” Need to define baseline precisely (e.g., model’s default serving mode). Also define what happens if baseline also not available (separate normal routing fallback).

Ravi Menon: Suggest: quantization fallback is orthogonal. If quant profile incompatible/unavailable -> serve with route’s normal model variant at baseline precision. If the model itself is unavailable -> normal routing fallback logic (existing).

Jordan Lee: We should explicitly state order of operations in ADR:
1) route -> model variant selection (existing)
2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.

Talia Benson: Action items before review:
- Priya/Jordan/Ravi: propose final decision on alias vs pinned profile id (sounds pinned)
- Noah/Mateo: propose runtime last-resort fallback behavior + metrics
- Naomi/Marcus: confirm RBAC/audit requirements (who can toggle, what gets logged)
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm Console flow: preview -> write pinned id -> show warnings

Priya Natarajan: I’ll add comments in ADR today.

Jordan Lee: I’ll comment with determinism language + avoid alias resolution at request time.

Noah Patel: I’ll add a section to ADR for runtime responsibilities vs policy responsibilities, and the “no re-query” rule.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add security notes. One ask: ensure profile artifacts are integrity checked (signing / checksum) and failures trigger fallback.

Talia Benson: Great. See you Thu unless conflicts. :thumbsup:

(DeployBot): Reminder: Architecture Review "Quantization Profile Enablement + Fallbacks" scheduled Thu 11:00 PT (Zoom link in calendar invite).

title: #eng-platform: 1739408123
preview:
Amara Diallo: quick q for RBAC v2: where do we want to enforce authz checks long-term — gateway vs service? I’m leaning gateway because consistency + perf, but worried about bypass/internal calls. Looking for a decision we can turn into an ADR.

Jordan Lee: tl;dr IMO service must be source of truth. Gateway-only is too easy to bypass (internal service-to-service, console talking directly in some flows, private installer hitting CP). Gateway can be defense-in-depth / early reject.

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane endpoints need checks in-process. We already have internal jobs calling the same handlers w/o going thru gateway.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also authn context differs. Gateway sees external identity, but service has to validate org/project/env scope anyway. If we push checks to gateway only, services will start trusting headers and that’s… spooky.

Amara Diallo: yup, the “trust an X-Authz-Result header” pattern makes me nervous. But I still want the gateway to block obvious stuff to keep load down + standardize error payloads.

Priya Natarajan: think of it as layers:
- gateway: coarse, fast, deny-by-default for annotated routes; consistent 403 semantics; metrics
- service: authoritative check w/ same shared library; required for any state-changing + sensitive reads
If there’s ever divergence, service wins.

Marcus Lin: security POV: checks MUST exist at the data boundary (service) or it’s not defensible in SOC2 / enterprise review. Gateway checks are great but not sufficient evidence.

Allison Grant: from observability side, having checks in service makes audit logging + “who/what/why denied” easier to capture with the action + resolved scope. Gateway doesn’t always know final resource.

Amara Diallo: ok. then performance question: do we expect every service to call a centralized authz service? or embed policy eval lib?

Jordan Lee: this is basically ADR-015. I’m in “embedded evaluator + shared policy bundle + safe caching” camp.
Central service = single point of failure + latency tax + hard in Private (customers don’t want a bunch of moving pieces).

Ethan Park: also network hops in private/onprem are unreliable. A central authz svc outage would brick the control plane.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: but embedded evaluator needs up-to-date policy + bindings. what’s the caching / invalidation plan? that’s where we keep getting stuck.

Logan Wright: if we do embedded, we need:
1) policy bundle versioning (immutable) + signed artifact
2) bindings stored in CP DB (or cache) w/ change notification
3) evaluator client cache keyed by (subject, action, scope, policy_version, bindings_version)
Then you can do bounded TTL + fast invalidation on role changes.

Jordan Lee: exactly. I don’t want “TTL only” because role removals should take effect quickly. Suggest:
- short TTL (e.g., 30s) for allow decisions
- shorter TTL (e.g., 5-10s) for deny decisions (to avoid annoying “still denied” after grant)
- hard invalidation when we see bindings_version bump for that org/project

Ines Carvalho: careful: scope is not always just org. For v2 we have org/project/env(/cluster for private). So invalidation needs to be scoped, otherwise one role edit flushes everything for an org and kills cache hit rate.

Amara Diallo: gateway cache: are we comfortable caching at gateway too? (it will call evaluator like everyone else)

Jordan Lee: yes, but only via the same evaluator client library. No bespoke gateway-only cache.

Marcus Lin: also important: cache must be scoped to tenant. Obvious, but we’ve been bitten before with global caches. Include org_id in the cache key always. :grimacing:

Allison Grant: + include region/cluster in key for Dedicated/Private so you don’t cross-contaminate.

Ethan Park: failure modes:
- if policy bundle fetch fails -> keep last-known-good (LKG) for N minutes, alert
- if bindings store read fails -> should we fail closed? at least for writes

Kaitlyn Nguyen: we need to be explicit about fail-open vs fail-closed. My proposal:
- write operations: fail closed (503 if evaluator unavailable vs 403)
- read operations: default fail closed too, but we can consider some non-sensitive reads fail-open only if explicitly annotated (probably not for v2)

Marcus Lin: agree, fail-open should be rare and very deliberate. If anything, we prefer 503 “authz unavailable” over incorrectly allowing.

Amara Diallo: gateway behavior if evaluator errors: return 503? (not 403). For consistency we can do:
- 403 = explicit deny
- 503 = authz system unavailable

Allison Grant: yes, and we should emit distinct metrics. We’ll need dashboards:
- authz_eval_latency_ms p50/p95/p99
- authz_decision_total{allow|deny|error}
- deny reason codes counts
- cache hit rate

Logan Wright: + add trace annotation so we can see where the latency is (gateway vs service). Otherwise we’ll argue about it forever.

Benji Okafor: another angle: even if gateway blocks, service still needs to protect itself against direct access (internal mesh, cronjobs, etc). So we should treat gateway check as optional optimization.

Amara Diallo: what about internal service-to-service calls where user context is propagated? ex: console -> API -> CP -> subservice.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: we should propagate a “subject” in a signed internal token (our existing service auth token) and do authz at the leaf service using that subject + action + scope. No “I already checked at gateway” short-circuiting.

Jordan Lee: +1. If we add an optimization later, it should be something like a short-lived signed authz assertion with policy_version embedded. But that’s v3 territory and easy to get wrong.

Priya Natarajan: keep it simple for v2:
- enforce in service
- optionally enforce in gateway too
- shared library + evaluator client cache
- clear semantics for errors
Let’s document and move.

rbac-bot: Reminder: ADR-015 draft exists in Confluence but not finalized. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-015+Centralized+policy+evaluation+vs+embedded (access may be restricted)

Connor O'Brien: release angle: if we rely on a central authz svc, rollouts become scary (can’t stage per-service). Embedded w/ policy bundle versioning is much easier to roll out behind flags.

Amara Diallo: makes sense. One more: streaming endpoints. gateway sees the upgrade, service streams tokens. where do we check?

Ethan Park: check at stream start in the service and bind the stream to the validated subject+scope. Don’t allow mid-stream “permission changed” to cut it off for v2 (too complex). But if the initial check is missing, that’s exactly how we get bypasses.

Marcus Lin: + add regression tests specifically for streaming routes. We already saw how easy it is to forget the check when it’s a different handler path.

Amara Diallo: ok action items:
- Jordan/Priya: can you update ADR-015 w/ this framing (service is source of truth; gateway optional defense-in-depth; embedded evaluator + safe caching; 403 vs 503 semantics)?
- I’ll align gateway middleware to call the shared evaluator client and standardize error responses.

Jordan Lee: yep. I’ll add a section on caching + invalidation triggers and explicitly call out “no trusting forwarded authz headers”.

Priya Natarajan: I’ll review + make sure we capture Private constraints (LKG policy, no hard dependency on an always-on central service).

Allison Grant: I’ll draft the metrics/events list and drop it in the ADR comments.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll note the subject propagation expectations + how we represent scope in the request context.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: thanks all

title: #eng-platform: 1739402017
preview:
Jordan Lee: Need to nail down HOLD semantics for Dedicated staged rollouts before we lock the state machine. Questions:
1) what does hold *actually* freeze (routing? gate eval? auto-promote/auto-abort?)
2) do we auto-timeout holds?
3) what does “resume” do to gate windows / samples?

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane wise we can represent `state=HELD` with metadata (who/when/reason). But we need deterministic behavior across policy engine + routing.

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side: hold should mean “stop changing things” BUT we still need an escape hatch if the canary is melting prod. I’m worried about a hold blocking auto-abort.

Benji Okafor: Current implementation sketch (not merged yet):
- routing stays pinned to current stage partition while held
- controller stops promotions
- gate evaluator still computes pass/fail/unknown
But unclear whether we *act* on failures while held.

Jordan Lee: My preference: HOLD freezes *actions* from the controller (no promote, no abort, no auto-rollback). We still evaluate gates to show evidence in Console.

Rafael Mendes: If we freeze abort as well, we need a clear “you’re holding a failing rollout” behavior. Otherwise we’ll see “held” used as an excuse to let a bad build linger.

Priya Natarajan: Can we define two concepts?
- HOLD: operator initiated, pauses automation
- SAFETY ABORT: independent kill-switch if safety gates are in “critical fail”
I don’t want hold to override safety.

Jordan Lee: I can live with that if it’s tightly scoped and deterministic. Like only gates tagged `safety=true` can fire while held.

Logan Wright: FWIW, if telemetry is the reason for HOLD (lag/outage), safety abort based on the same telemetry signals won’t help. But for stuff like 429 spikes / request drops from the router, those are near-real-time.

Hannah Rivera: UX question: do we show “Held (Safety monitoring still active)”? Otherwise customers assume nothing can happen.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Also RBAC: who can put a rollout on hold vs resume vs force-abort? We’ll need to align with the admin guardrails work.

Ethan Park: Proposing concrete semantics:
- Manual HOLD transitions `RUNNING -> HELD`.
- While HELD:
  - routing stays as-is (keep stage traffic split / partition selection frozen)
  - NO auto-promote
  - NO auto-rollback/abort *except* Safety Abort gates
  - gate evaluation continues and is displayed (pass/fail/unknown)
- Safety Abort: if any `safety=true` gate is failing for >X mins, we can `HELD -> ABORTED` automatically.
Thoughts?

Rafael Mendes: +1. Safety gates should include things like error rate, 429/drop, OOM crashloop, saturation/headroom. Latency p99 alone maybe not safety unless extreme.

Jordan Lee: We should define “critical fail” precisely or we’ll get inconsistent behavior.
Maybe:
- safety gate has `severity=critical` and `min_duration` (e.g., 10m)
- on critical fail, action is `auto_abort` even during hold
Everything else is “informational” during hold.

Benji Okafor: Works for control plane. We can store per-gate config already. Something like:
- `gates[].mode = {blocking|informational}`
- `gates[].safety_abort_enabled = true/false`
Then hold checks `safety_abort_enabled` gates only.

Priya Natarajan: Keep MVP simpler: one boolean flag per gate: `safety=true`. Default safety gates for Dedicated should be limited set. Paula is doing thresholds but we can define the behavior.

Hannah Rivera: Console: when held, the promote button should be disabled and replaced w/ “Resume rollout”. Also show “Hold reason” and “Held by” + “hold expires” if we implement timeouts.

Jordan Lee: Let’s talk timeouts. I think we need them. People will hold and forget.

Ethan Park: Control plane can support `hold_until` (timestamp). But what is the system behavior at expiration?
- auto-resume?
- auto-abort?
- just alert?

Rafael Mendes: NOT auto-resume. That’s a change-management nightmare. Also auto-resume could re-trigger automation during an incident.
I’d rather: hold expires => we *auto-abort* (rollback to previous stable stage) unless explicitly extended.

Hannah Rivera: That’s going to surprise people if they just wanted to pause overnight.

Jordan Lee: Alternative: hold expires => move to “HELD_EXPIRED” and keep frozen but page the right oncall.
But that’s also messy.

Priya Natarajan: We need something that’s safe by default and easy to explain.
How about:
- HOLD always requires explicit RESUME. No auto-resume.
- `hold_until` is an SLA/guardrail.
- On expiration:
  - if rollout is currently on a non-100% stage (i.e. still partial) => auto-abort/rollback to previous stable stage
  - if rollout is already at 100% stage (rare to hold there) => do nothing, just alert
This avoids leaving partial partitions around forever.

Rafael Mendes: That’s aligned with capacity fragmentation concerns. A held 10% stage is basically reserving a split indefinitely.

Benji Okafor: We can implement “partial stage” check as `current_stage_index < final_stage_index`. Auto-abort can revert routing to previous stage partition (or “baseline config”), per rollback ADR.

Jordan Lee: What’s default `hold_until` duration?

Rafael Mendes: For SRE, 2h is too short. I’d say 24h default, max 72h. We can extend explicitly.

Hannah Rivera: UX wise: default 24h is fine as long as it’s visible and extendable.

Logan Wright: Telemetry outages sometimes last 30–60m. 24h seems safe.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC proposal:
- `rollouts.hold`: Dedicated Operator + Redwood SRE + Customer Admin (if we allow customer-initiated holds)
- `rollouts.resume`: same
- `rollouts.extend_hold`: same
- `rollouts.force_abort`: Redwood SRE + Dedicated Operator (customer admin maybe no, depends)
We should at least ensure hold/resume events are audited with reason.

Ethan Park: +1 on audit fields. For API:
- POST /dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollouts/{rollout_id}:hold {reason, hold_until?, idempotency_key?}
- POST ...:resume {reason, idempotency_key?}
Also store `held_by`, `held_reason`, `held_at`, `hold_until`.

Jordan Lee: Resume semantics: on resume, do we restart gate evaluation windows? I’d say YES. Otherwise you can “hold” for 3h and then resume and immediately get a stale “pass” computed over a window that included pre-hold.

Logan Wright: Agree. Gate evaluator can treat `resume_at` as a new “stage epoch” boundary.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: we already have `stage_entered_at`. If we resume without changing stage, we can set `stage_epoch++` / `stage_entered_at = now` even though stage index doesn’t change.

Rafael Mendes: Also minimum sample size: reset on resume too.

Hannah Rivera: Console messaging: “Resuming will restart gate evaluation timers for the current stage.”

Priya Natarajan: Summarizing decisions (unless objections):
1) HOLD freezes routing config at the current stage (no traffic split changes).
2) While HELD:
   - auto-promote disabled
   - gate evaluation continues for visibility
   - auto-abort disabled except for safety gates (`safety=true`) that are in critical fail for configured duration
3) HOLD has `hold_until` with default 24h. No auto-resume.
4) If `hold_until` expires while rollout is not at final stage => auto-abort/rollback to previous stable stage + page/alert.
5) RESUME requires explicit action and resets gate evaluation windows + min sample counters for the current stage (new epoch).
6) All hold/resume/auto-timeout actions must emit audit events w/ actor + reason.

Jordan Lee: Looks good. I’ll update the policy engine behavior doc + add “stage epoch on resume” to the evaluator.

Ethan Park: I’ll capture this in the control plane API notes + state machine comment. Benji can implement `hold_until` + expiry worker.

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll wire up:
- state transition validation (can only hold from RUNNING)
- expiry job => ABORT with reason “hold_timeout”
- new audit event types: rollout.held, rollout.resumed, rollout.hold_timeout_aborted

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Please ping me when you have the exact audit payload shape; I want `reason` mandatory and `hold_until` included.

Rafael Mendes: Also please make sure auto-abort on hold-timeout routes to oncall in alerting. I’ll add runbook steps for “held expired / timeout abort”.

Hannah Rivera: I’ll add UX copy for “Safety monitoring still active” + show countdown timer + extend flow.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Next: decide which gates default to `safety=true` for v1. (separate thread w/ Paula + SRE)

title: #eng-platform: 1739378821
preview:
Ethan Park: Question for folks thinking about route-level enablement: should the route config allow setting an explicit quant profile ID (ex: `qp_llama3_70b_int8_v3`) OR do we only allow aliases like `recommended` / `latest`?

Mina Haddad: My gut says explicit IDs only. Aliases feel like a footgun unless we define *exactly* when they resolve. Determinism + audit gets messy if `recommended` changes out from under you.

Ravi Menon: +1. Policy engine needs deterministic behavior. If alias resolves at request time, then two requests with same route config could apply different profiles depending on publish cadence.

Benji Okafor: From API ergonomics POV customers will ask for “just give me the best one”. Aliases are attractive. But we can do it in Console UX without storing it as alias maybe?

Jordan Lee: The determinism requirement is pretty strict. If we support `recommended`, I’d prefer it resolves at *write time* (set route config), and we store the concrete profile id. That keeps routing/runtime deterministic and audit diffs meaningful.

Ethan Park: Right, the field is currently drafted as `quantization_profile_ref`. We’ve got `default` (meaning baseline precision) and then a ref. If we allow alias, the question is whether we persist `alias:recommended` or persist resolved id.

Priya Natarajan: We should avoid “silent change” semantics. Customers will be unhappy if output shifts because we published a new recommended profile and their route updated implicitly.

Chloe Martin: Console team concern: if a user clicks “Enable recommended profile”, they’ll expect it to track updates. But if we store resolved id, then “recommended” is basically a convenience selector, not an ongoing policy.

Mina Haddad: Convenience selector is fine. If we want tracking, that’s a different feature (and needs explicit consent + change control). Also for compliance customers, implicit changes are a no-go.

Benji Okafor: Another option: allow alias in API but require a `pinned: true/false` flag? If pinned false, then it’s dynamic. But that’s basically v2.

Ravi Menon: Dynamic alias means policy engine has to re-resolve on each config refresh and we need to answer “which version was applied for request X” reliably. That is doable if runtime logs include resolved `quant_profile_id`, but still breaks “same config => same behavior”.

Jordan Lee: Also impacts rollback. If alias points to v4 and quality regresses, you need to repoint alias globally or coordinate. With explicit IDs, you can just flip route back to baseline or prior known-good.

Ethan Park: How do we handle multi-region / dedicated pools? If someone sets `recommended`, it might differ per GPU SKU. Alias might resolve to different profile ids depending on region/hardware.

Mina Haddad: Exactly. “Recommended” is not a single thing unless scoped by model + hardware class + max context regime. If we do alias, we’ll need to define the resolution key.

Chloe Martin: Console can still show “Recommended for your current deployment (H100 class)” and store the resolved id. If user later changes region/tier and that profile becomes incompatible, policy should fallback to baseline and show warning.

Priya Natarajan: That sounds aligned with guardrails. Store explicit id in route config. Policy engine checks compatibility; if incompatible, fallback. No dynamic alias.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: Works for API too: accept `quantization_profile_ref` as either a concrete id OR a special token `recommended` in the *request*, but the server will translate it to concrete id before persisting. We just need to document that behavior.

Ravi Menon: Important: we should be explicit that `recommended` is “resolve now” not “follow updates”. And the audit log should include both (requested alias + stored id) maybe.

Jordan Lee: +1. For audit: record `requested_ref=recommended`, `resolved_profile_id=qp_...v3`, `resolution_scope=model+variant+gpu_class`.

Ethan Park: ok. I’ll update the config service spec accordingly: route settings accept `default` | `profile_id` | `recommended` (server-resolved), but storage is always `default` or concrete `profile_id`.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a UI string like “This will pin the current recommended profile version; future updates won’t apply unless you change it.”? Otherwise people will assume it auto-updates.

Priya Natarajan: Yes, and that message is important for enterprise change control.

Mina Haddad: Also: if a profile gets deprecated/blocked, we should return a clear error on enable and/or automatically fallback with a big warning. Prefer blocking enable.

Ravi Menon: For existing routes pointing at deprecated id, fallback + metric `quant_profile_resolution=fallback_deprecated` + alert.

Benji Okafor: I’ll sync with Jordan on error codes and how the API surfaces “alias resolved” vs “explicit id accepted”.

Jordan Lee: I’ll drop a note in the ADR draft section on alias semantics: allowed as input, never persisted; deterministic storage; no silent upgrades.

Ethan Park: Cool. Action items: Benji update OpenAPI + client types, Chloe/Tessa align on Console copy, Jordan add to ADR. Thanks all.

Chloe Martin: :white_check_mark: will add to Console ticket notes.

Priya Natarajan: :thumbsup:

title: #eng-security: 1739319001
preview:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off the naming convention thread. We need something consistent before we lock the permission registry + start annotating endpoints. Current state is a mess (some `org.admin`, some `manage_keys`, some hardcoded strings in gateway).

Marcus Lin: Proposals I’ve heard:
- `org.api_keys.rotate`
- `controlplane.api_keys.rotate`
- `redwood.org.api_keys.rotate`
- `rbac:org:api_keys:rotate` (pls no)

Marcus Lin: Main questions:
1) separator (`.` vs `:`)
2) namespacing (product/service vs domain)
3) versioning (in string vs metadata)
4) verb set (create/update/delete/manage?)

Jordan Lee: +1. From policy engine side I strongly prefer `.` separated, lowercase, deterministic segments. Easy to lint + match prefixes.

Jordan Lee: I’d vote for: `<domain>.<resource>.<action>` where domain = customer-facing conceptual area (org/project/deployment/audit/etc), NOT internal service name.

Jordan Lee: Examples:
```
org.users.invite
org.roles.update
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
audit_logs.export
```

Naomi Feldman: PM angle: agree on conceptual domain. If we namespace by service (`gateway.*`, `controlplane.*`) it’s going to leak internals into docs + customer configs.

Priya Natarajan: +1 on not coupling to services. But we do need to avoid collisions as scope expands (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). “deployment” vs “cluster” vs “environment” will matter.

Amara Diallo: Gateway needs something that can be attached as an annotation + checked quickly. Dots are fine. Please don’t do colons; it interacts weirdly with a couple of our log parsers and header formats.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For SSO/group mapping (esp Private) customers will see these strings in some capacity (Console permission viewer, docs). Readability matters.

Ethan Park: One pain point: some actions are “read” vs “list” vs “get”. Can we standardize verbs? If we have both `read` and `get` floating around it’ll explode.

Jordan Lee: Verbs: I’d propose a constrained set:
- `get` (single)
- `list`
- `create`
- `update`
- `delete`
- `rotate` (special case)
- `enable` / `disable` (special)
- `export` (audit/log export)

Jordan Lee: And avoid `manage` unless it’s truly composite. `manage` becomes a permission bucket and breaks least privilege.

Marcus Lin: Agree. We should push people away from `manage_*` unless we’re intentionally creating a coarse role template.

Naomi Feldman: What about `view` vs `read`? Some UX language says “View audit logs”. But for the permission string I’m fine with `get/list`.

Amara Diallo: We should align with API patterns: HTTP GET on `/audit-logs` maps to `audit_logs.list`, GET on `/audit-logs/{id}` maps to `audit_logs.get`. Makes it easy in codegen.

Priya Natarajan: +1. That gives us a clean mapping rule.

Brian Murphy: Jumping in — docs team will thank you if we do `get/list/create/update/delete` and avoid synonyms.

Marcus Lin: Versioning question: do we ever put `v1` in the permission string? e.g. `org.api_keys.rotate.v1`?

Jordan Lee: Please no. If permissions are part of customer contracts, changing the string is painful. Better: permission ID is stable, and we add metadata:
- `introduced_at`
- `deprecated_at`
- `replaced_by`

Jordan Lee: Then policy bundle tooling can emit warnings if you still use deprecated perms.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: If we don’t version in the string, how do we handle semantics changes? Like splitting one permission into two.

Jordan Lee: That’s a migration problem, not a naming problem. We keep old perm working (maybe mapped internally) while new perms exist. Eventually deprecate + remove with a long runway.

Naomi Feldman: I like that. For enterprise questionnaires, we can say “permissions are stable identifiers, with explicit deprecation policy”.

Priya Natarajan: +1 but we need a hard rule: never change semantics of an existing permission. If semantics change, create a new permission and deprecate the old.

Marcus Lin: That’s basically what ADR-014 should state.

Amara Diallo: From gateway perspective, having stable strings helps caching too. If we embed version in the string we’ll end up with multiple checks and weird dual-read behavior.

Ethan Park: Namespacing question: do we want `org.*` and `project.*` etc. Or `console.*`? Some console-only actions (like “view billing page”) shouldn’t be permissions at all IMO.

Naomi Feldman: Console page access should be derived from API permissions, not new console-only permissions. Otherwise we create UI drift.

Marcus Lin: +1. “Sensitive actions” should map to actual state changes / data access. UI routes should call APIs and get denied there.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For Private installer/bootstrap we might need a couple of “platform install” actions that aren’t customer API actions.

Priya Natarajan: That’s still a domain though. Maybe `platform.install.bootstrap` or `private_deployments.*`?

Jordan Lee: We can use a `platform.*` domain for operator-only actions. Keep it minimal and clearly documented as operator scope.

Marcus Lin: Ok. Another naming nit: pluralization and underscores. `api_keys` vs `apikeys` vs `api-keys`.

Jordan Lee: Use snake_case for multiword segments (`api_keys`, `audit_logs`, `routing_policies`). All lowercase. No hyphens.

Amara Diallo: +1. Hyphens are annoying in some tooling. Snake_case is easiest.

Naomi Feldman: So proposed format:
`<domain>.<resource>.<action>`
- `domain`: `org`, `project`, `deployment`, `audit_logs`, `billing`, `platform` (?)
- `resource`: snake_case
- `action`: from allowed verbs

Naomi Feldman: Small question: is `audit_logs` a domain or resource? Example `audit_logs.export` has only 2 segments.

Jordan Lee: Good catch. I’d rather keep 3 segments always. So domain `audit`, resource `logs`, action `export` -> `audit.logs.export`.

Marcus Lin: That reads weird but is consistent.

Ethan Park: Alternative: treat “audit_logs” as resource and have domain `org` or `project`? Like `org.audit_logs.export`.

Priya Natarajan: I like `org.audit_logs.export` because audit logs are scoped to org anyway (at least today). And we might have `project.audit_logs.export` later if we add project-level audit streams.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. Also makes scoping clearer in docs (“This role can export org audit logs”).

Jordan Lee: Works for me. Then domain is the scope container, not the service.

Jordan Lee: So:
```
org.audit_logs.list
org.audit_logs.export
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
deployment.model_pins.update
```

Marcus Lin: That’s pretty solid.

Amara Diallo: For routing, are we sure it’s `project.routing_policies.update` and not `project.routing.update`? “policy” is an overloaded word here.

Priya Natarajan: We should match the actual object name in the control plane: “routing policy” is the thing with fallback + region weights, so `routing_policies` is fine.

Naomi Feldman: Another question: do we include “environment” as a domain or as a scope on the resource? Like `project.environments.update` vs `environment.routing_policies.update`.

Jordan Lee: Naming doesn’t encode scope. Scope is in the evaluation input (org_id/project_id/env_id/cluster_id). Permission string should not vary based on scope.

Jordan Lee: So you can have `project.routing_policies.update` evaluated at env scope depending on request context. (Policy engine checks binding scope).

Marcus Lin: Right, scope semantics are separate.

Brian Murphy: For docs, we should include a “permission naming rules” section + a “how to request a new permission” template. Otherwise every team will invent new patterns.

Marcus Lin: Action items:
- Jordan: update ADR-014 draft with:
  - dot-separated, lowercase, snake_case segments
  - no service names in permission IDs
  - no version suffix in string; versioning via metadata + deprecation policy
  - verb set + guidance against `manage`
- Naomi: sanity-check against customer-facing terminology + console UX (permission viewer/search)
- Amara/Ethan: confirm gateway + control plane annotations can support `org.*` / `project.*` patterns cleanly

Priya Natarajan: +1. Also add lint rules in the permission registry PR so we fail CI on format violations.

Jordan Lee: On it. I’ll drop a PR comment in `pr-28134` once I’ve updated the linter rules.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: Also please reserve `internal.*` or `platform.*` now, so we don’t end up with random “ops_*” strings later.

Marcus Lin: Good call. Let’s reserve `platform.*` for operator-only actions.

Naomi Feldman: Works. One more: should we prefix everything with `redwood.`?

Jordan Lee: I don’t think so. Adds noise, no extra value. These aren’t globally unique identifiers; they’re scoped to Redwood policy bundle.

Priya Natarajan: Agree. Keep short.

Marcus Lin: Consensus seems: no `redwood.` prefix.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. In UI the shorter the better.

Marcus Lin: Cool. I’ll mark this thread as the input to ADR-014 and link it in Confluence. Thanks all.
text:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off the naming convention thread. We need something consistent before we lock the permission registry + start annotating endpoints. Current state is a mess (some `org.admin`, some `manage_keys`, some hardcoded strings in gateway).

Marcus Lin: Proposals I’ve heard:
- `org.api_keys.rotate`
- `controlplane.api_keys.rotate`
- `redwood.org.api_keys.rotate`
- `rbac:org:api_keys:rotate` (pls no)

Marcus Lin: Main questions:
1) separator (`.` vs `:`)
2) namespacing (product/service vs domain)
3) versioning (in string vs metadata)
4) verb set (create/update/delete/manage?)

Jordan Lee: +1. From policy engine side I strongly prefer `.` separated, lowercase, deterministic segments. Easy to lint + match prefixes.

Jordan Lee: I’d vote for: `<domain>.<resource>.<action>` where domain = customer-facing conceptual area (org/project/deployment/audit/etc), NOT internal service name.

Jordan Lee: Examples:
```
org.users.invite
org.roles.update
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
audit_logs.export
```

Naomi Feldman: PM angle: agree on conceptual domain. If we namespace by service (`gateway.*`, `controlplane.*`) it’s going to leak internals into docs + customer configs.

Priya Natarajan: +1 on not coupling to services. But we do need to avoid collisions as scope expands (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). “deployment” vs “cluster” vs “environment” will matter.

Amara Diallo: Gateway needs something that can be attached as an annotation + checked quickly. Dots are fine. Please don’t do colons; it interacts weirdly with a couple of our log parsers and header formats.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For SSO/group mapping (esp Private) customers will see these strings in some capacity (Console permission viewer, docs). Readability matters.

Ethan Park: One pain point: some actions are “read” vs “list” vs “get”. Can we standardize verbs? If we have both `read` and `get` floating around it’ll explode.

Jordan Lee: Verbs: I’d propose a constrained set:
- `get` (single)
- `list`
- `create`
- `update`
- `delete`
- `rotate` (special case)
- `enable` / `disable` (special)
- `export` (audit/log export)

Jordan Lee: And avoid `manage` unless it’s truly composite. `manage` becomes a permission bucket and breaks least privilege.

Marcus Lin: Agree. We should push people away from `manage_*` unless we’re intentionally creating a coarse role template.

Naomi Feldman: What about `view` vs `read`? Some UX language says “View audit logs”. But for the permission string I’m fine with `get/list`.

Amara Diallo: We should align with API patterns: HTTP GET on `/audit-logs` maps to `audit_logs.list`, GET on `/audit-logs/{id}` maps to `audit_logs.get`. Makes it easy in codegen.

Jordan Lee: That’s a migration problem, not a naming problem. We keep old perm working (maybe mapped internally) while new perms exist. Eventually deprecate + remove with a long runway.

Naomi Feldman: I like that. For enterprise questionnaires, we can say “permissions are stable identifiers, with explicit deprecation policy”.

Priya Natarajan: +1 but we need a hard rule: never change semantics of an existing permission. If semantics change, create a new permission and deprecate the old.

Marcus Lin: That’s basically what ADR-014 should state.

Amara Diallo: From gateway perspective, having stable strings helps caching too. If we embed version in the string we’ll end up with multiple checks and weird dual-read behavior.

Ethan Park: Namespacing question: do we want `org.*` and `project.*` etc. Or `console.*`? Some console-only actions (like “view billing page”) shouldn’t be permissions at all IMO.

Naomi Feldman: Console page access should be derived from API permissions, not new console-only permissions. Otherwise we create UI drift.

Marcus Lin: +1. “Sensitive actions” should map to actual state changes / data access. UI routes should call APIs and get denied there.

title: #eng-platform: 1739318220
preview:
Benji Okafor: seeing flaky behavior in the promote endpoint under retries. In the e2e test we sometimes “double-promote” from stage 1 -> 2 -> 3 when Console retries after a 502. We need to lock down idempotency + optimistic concurrency before beta.

Benji Okafor: current behavior: `POST /v1/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/rollout_plans/{plan_id}:promote` is not idempotent. If Console times out but server commits, retry does another promote.

Jordan Lee: +1, we should treat promote like a write action that must be at-most-once per client intent.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
1) require `Idempotency-Key` header for action endpoints (`promote`, `abort`, `hold`, `force_promote`).
2) require an expected version for optimistic concurrency (either `If-Match: W/"plan_version"` or explicit `expected_plan_version` in body).
3) server stores the idempotency record keyed by `(plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key)` and returns the same response on replay.

Jordan Lee: if same idempotency key is reused w/ different payload (e.g. different `reason` or `requested_stage_index`), return 400.

Ethan Park: +1 overall. I’m a little worried about “required” idempotency key for internal callers. Are we ok with making it mandatory now or do we do “strongly recommended + enforce for Console”?

Jada Williams: Console will do retries automatically (our gateway + browser retry on flaky networks). If it’s not mandatory, we’ll miss it in some path and we’ll be back here.

Chloe Martin: fwiw Console backend can always send one. We can generate a UUID per button click and persist it client-side while the request is in-flight.

Benji Okafor: +1 for mandatory. The failures we saw were from the backend job that replays on 5xx.

Jordan Lee: also: optimistic concurrency alone doesn’t solve the retry case. If request #1 succeeded and bumped `plan_version`, request #2 (retry) might use a stale version and fail with 409, which is “safe”, but the UI shows an error even though it promoted.

Jordan Lee: idempotency gives us “retry returns success with same result” vs “retry returns conflict”. Much nicer.

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Ethan Park: what’s the exact contract? Example?

Jordan Lee: something like:
```
POST ...:promote
Idempotency-Key: 6b10f5d4-0b18-4d90-bb5a-9b3a86d7a0c9
If-Match: W/"17"

{ "reason": "canary looks good" }

200
{ "plan_id": "rp_123", "previous_stage": 1, "current_stage": 2, "plan_version": 18 }
```

Jordan Lee: If client retries with same key, we return the same 200 payload (even if If-Match is stale / missing on retry).

Jordan Lee: If client sends different key but stale If-Match, return `409` with current plan state:
```
409
{ "error": "version_conflict", "current_plan_version": 18, "current_stage": 2 }
```

Benji Okafor: question: should idempotency bypass authz? (i.e., if user A promoted, and user B replays the same key, do we return the cached response?)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pls do NOT bypass authz. Cached result should still require caller to be authorized for the action (at least “can view plan”); otherwise you can leak that a promote happened + stage index.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’d suggest: store `actor_id` / `actor_type` with idempotency record and require the replaying request to match the same actor, or at minimum same org + same permission level. Strongest is “must be same actor”.

Jordan Lee: +1 on same actor. If Console retries, it’s same service principal anyway.

Chloe Martin: careful: Console backend uses service-to-service token, but the end-user actor is in `X-Redwood-Acting-User`. We sometimes replay with the same service principal but a different acting user depending on session refresh.

Chloe Martin: could we scope “same actor” to the service principal + org, and treat `acting_user` as audit-only?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok with that as long as:
- the cached response does not get returned to a different org
- and we still log the actual actor for the *first* commit
- replays should not emit additional audit events

Benji Okafor: +1, main need is preventing double side-effects.

Priya Natarajan: for v1: enforce that the idempotency record is scoped to `(org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)` where `auth_principal_id` is the service principal (Console backend / operator tooling). Don’t include acting user in the match requirement.

Priya Natarajan: Kaitlyn can we live with that for beta? If yes, we’ll document.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, with the constraints above. Also ensure the response doesn’t include anything beyond what the principal could fetch via GET anyway.

Ethan Park: storage details: do we need a new table?

Benji Okafor: I can add `rollout_action_requests` (or similar) with:
- org_id
- plan_id
- action_type
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal_id
- request_hash
- status (in_progress|completed|failed)
- response_body (or key fields)
- created_at, completed_at
Unique index on (org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)

Jordan Lee: +1. For “in_progress”: if two requests race with same key, second should block or return 409/202.

Jordan Lee: I prefer: second returns `409 idempotency_in_progress` w/ `Retry-After: 1` so client can poll. But Console probably just retries anyway.

Chloe Martin: Console backend can handle `409 idempotency_in_progress` and retry with backoff. Alternatively we can return `202`.

Ethan Park: 202 semantics are confusing here. I’d do 409 w/ specific code.

Benji Okafor: ack.

Benji Okafor: also, how do we prevent *different keys* from double-promoting concurrently? E.g. two operators click promote at same time.

Jordan Lee: that’s optimistic concurrency: the action must be evaluated against a known plan_version inside a transaction.

Jordan Lee: DB transaction: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` on plan row, verify expected_plan_version (or If-Match), compute next stage, write plan, insert audit event, then commit. If expected version mismatch: 409.

Ethan Park: I’m leaning toward body field instead of If-Match just to keep it consistent with our other internal APIs.

Jada Williams: Console prefers If-Match, but we can do either. We already parse ETags on some endpoints.

Benji Okafor: if we do body field, can we still return ETag on GET? would be nice.

Jordan Lee: can do both: accept either `If-Match` OR `expected_plan_version`. If both present and disagree => 400.

Priya Natarajan: +1 accept either. Console can move to If-Match, internal tooling can keep body param.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: make sure the idempotency key can’t be replayed forever. TTL?

Benji Okafor: I was thinking keep records 7d for debugging/audit correlation.

Jordan Lee: 7d is fine, but also cap table growth. Maybe 24h for “dedupe guarantee” and best-effort after? But semantics get weird.

Ethan Park: for action endpoints, we really only need short window. But support will love having it around.

Priya Natarajan: decision: keep for 7d, but only guarantee replay for 24h. After 24h, server *may* return 404 for the idempotency replay and client should treat it as non-retriable. (Console retries will all be within minutes anyway.)

Chloe Martin: can we avoid the “may” language and just keep full replay for 7d? Implementation is the same if we store response.

Benji Okafor: storing response body for 7d is ok, it’s small.

Jordan Lee: I’d rather keep deterministic: if record exists, replay forever until GC. So “guarantee” equals retention. If we retain 7d, we guarantee 7d.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: retention for idempotency record is not audit log retention, but ok. Just ensure it doesn’t include sensitive payload beyond what’s needed.

Priya Natarajan: ok, simplify: guarantee for 7d. We can tune later.

Ethan Park: edge case: first request fails after partially executing (e.g. audit insert succeeded but plan update failed). Idempotency record should mark failed and allow retry?

Jordan Lee: action must be atomic. Plan update + audit insert in same txn. If txn rolls back, idempotency record should not be marked completed.

Benji Okafor: but idempotency record insert needs to happen before we start, otherwise races.

Jordan Lee: pattern:
1) start txn
2) insert idempotency row with status=in_progress (unique constraint)
3) lock plan row
4) validate + perform action
5) update idempotency row status=completed + store response
6) commit

Jordan Lee: if txn fails, idempotency row insert rolls back too.

Benji Okafor: makes sense.

Jada Williams: also note: promote is not instantaneous from data plane perspective, but control plane state change is. So idempotency is about control plane state transition, not “all pods updated”.

Ethan Park: yup.

Chloe Martin: can we include `resulting_action_id` in response so Console can fetch gate evidence / timeline by action id?

Benji Okafor: good idea. Will generate `ra_...` action id. Also helps correlate to audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please ensure audit event includes:
- action_id
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal
- acting user (if present)
- old_stage/new_stage
- reason
But again: replay should NOT emit audit twice.

Jordan Lee: +1. We should also include `idempotency_replayed=true` flag in response headers? maybe too much.

Priya Natarajan: no need. But add a debug log line when replay occurs.

Ethan Park: so final behaviors:
- same key => same response (200 or 409?), no extra side effects
- different key + same expected version => one wins, other 409
- missing key => 400
- missing expected version => ???

Jordan Lee: missing expected version should be 400 too. Otherwise two parallel promotes from different clients can both succeed sequentially, which is unexpected.

Jada Williams: agree. Promote should be explicit about what you think current stage/version is.

Benji Okafor: ok: require BOTH `Idempotency-Key` AND expected version.

Priya Natarajan: decision: required for all action endpoints.

Priya Natarajan: Benji can you capture in the PR description + update the API docs in the service doc? (Also ping Connor so QA knows what to test.)

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll add test cases:
- retry same key returns same `action_id` + stage
- concurrent promote with different keys -> one 200, one 409
- replay with different payload -> 400

Chloe Martin: I’ll update Console backend to always send both headers. For expected version we’ll use ETag from GET plan.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please add one more test: replay with different auth principal should NOT return cached response (should 409/404). We can decide exact code, but shouldn’t leak.

Jordan Lee: suggest 409 `idempotency_key_conflict` if key exists for different principal within same org/plan. That’s safe.

Benji Okafor: ok.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: thanks all

Priya Natarajan: :thumbsup: ship it, but behind the feature flag until Console is updated.

Benji Okafor: (deploy bot voice) will do.

Chloe Martin: :eyes: I’ll coordinate rollout order with release train so we don’t break existing clients.

```
{ "reason": "canary looks good" }

200
{ "plan_id": "rp_123", "previous_stage": 1, "current_stage": 2, "plan_version": 18 }
```

Jordan Lee: If client retries with same key, we return the same 200 payload (even if If-Match is stale / missing on retry).

Jordan Lee: If client sends different key but stale If-Match, return `409` with current plan state:
```
409
{ "error": "version_conflict", "current_plan_version": 18, "current_stage": 2 }
```

Benji Okafor: question: should idempotency bypass authz? (i.e., if user A promoted, and user B replays the same key, do we return the cached response?)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pls do NOT bypass authz. Cached result should still require caller to be authorized for the action (at least “can view plan”); otherwise you can leak that a promote happened + stage index.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’d suggest: store `actor_id` / `actor_type` with idempotency record and require the replaying request to match the same actor, or at minimum same org + same permission level. Strongest is “must be same actor”.

Jordan Lee: +1 on same actor. If Console retries, it’s same service principal anyway.

Chloe Martin: careful: Console backend uses service-to-service token, but the end-user actor is in `X-Redwood-Acting-User`. We sometimes replay with the same service principal but a different acting user depending on session refresh.

Chloe Martin: could we scope “same actor” to the service principal + org, and treat `acting_user` as audit-only?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok with that as long as:
- the cached response does not get returned to a different org
- and we still log the actual actor for the *first* commit
- replays should not emit additional audit events

Benji Okafor: +1, main need is preventing double side-effects.

Priya Natarajan: for v1: enforce that the idempotency record is scoped to `(org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)` where `auth_principal_id` is the service principal (Console backend / operator tooling). Don’t include acting user in the match requirement.

Priya Natarajan: Kaitlyn can we live with that for beta? If yes, we’ll document.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, with the constraints above. Also ensure the response doesn’t include anything beyond what the principal could fetch via GET anyway.

Ethan Park: storage details: do we need a new table?

Benji Okafor: I can add `rollout_action_requests` (or similar) with:
- org_id
- plan_id
- action_type
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal_id
- request_hash
- status (in_progress|completed|failed)
- response_body (or key fields)
- created_at, completed_at
Unique index on (org_id, plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key, auth_principal_id)

Jordan Lee: +1. For “in_progress”: if two requests race with same key, second should block or return 409/202.

Jordan Lee: I prefer: second returns `409 idempotency_in_progress` w/ `Retry-After: 1` so client can poll. But Console probably just retries anyway.

Chloe Martin: Console backend can handle `409 idempotency_in_progress` and retry with backoff. Alternatively we can return `202`.

Ethan Park: 202 semantics are confusing here. I’d do 409 w/ specific code.

Benji Okafor: ack.

Benji Okafor: also, how do we prevent *different keys* from double-promoting concurrently? E.g. two operators click promote at same time.

Jordan Lee: that’s optimistic concurrency: the action must be evaluated against a known plan_version inside a transaction.

Jordan Lee: DB transaction: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` on plan row, verify expected_plan_version (or If-Match), compute next stage, write plan, insert audit event, then commit. If expected version mismatch: 409.

Ethan Park: I’m leaning toward body field instead of If-Match just to keep it consistent with our other internal APIs.

Jada Williams: Console prefers If-Match, but we can do either. We already parse ETags on some endpoints.

Benji Okafor: if we do body field, can we still return ETag on GET? would be nice.

Jordan Lee: can do both: accept either `If-Match` OR `expected_plan_version`. If both present and disagree => 400.

Priya Natarajan: +1 accept either. Console can move to If-Match, internal tooling can keep body param.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: make sure the idempotency key can’t be replayed forever. TTL?

Benji Okafor: I was thinking keep records 7d for debugging/audit correlation.

Jordan Lee: 7d is fine, but also cap table growth. Maybe 24h for “dedupe guarantee” and best-effort after? But semantics get weird.

Ethan Park: for action endpoints, we really only need short window. But support will love having it around.

Priya Natarajan: decision: keep for 7d, but only guarantee replay for 24h. After 24h, server *may* return 404 for the idempotency replay and client should treat it as non-retriable. (Console retries will all be within minutes anyway.)

Chloe Martin: can we avoid the “may” language and just keep full replay for 7d? Implementation is the same if we store response.

Benji Okafor: storing response body for 7d is ok, it’s small.

Jordan Lee: I’d rather keep deterministic: if record exists, replay forever until GC. So “guarantee” equals retention. If we retain 7d, we guarantee 7d.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: retention for idempotency record is not audit log retention, but ok. Just ensure it doesn’t include sensitive payload beyond what’s needed.

Priya Natarajan: ok, simplify: guarantee for 7d. We can tune later.

Ethan Park: edge case: first request fails after partially executing (e.g. audit insert succeeded but plan update failed). Idempotency record should mark failed and allow retry?

Jordan Lee: action must be atomic. Plan update + audit insert in same txn. If txn rolls back, idempotency record should not be marked completed.

Benji Okafor: but idempotency record insert needs to happen before we start, otherwise races.

Jordan Lee: pattern:
1) start txn
2) insert idempotency row with status=in_progress (unique constraint)
3) lock plan row
4) validate + perform action
5) update idempotency row status=completed + store response
6) commit

Jordan Lee: if txn fails, idempotency row insert rolls back too.

Benji Okafor: makes sense.

Jada Williams: also note: promote is not instantaneous from data plane perspective, but control plane state change is. So idempotency is about control plane state transition, not “all pods updated”.

Ethan Park: yup.

Chloe Martin: can we include `resulting_action_id` in response so Console can fetch gate evidence / timeline by action id?

Benji Okafor: good idea. Will generate `ra_...` action id. Also helps correlate to audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please ensure audit event includes:
- action_id
- idempotency_key
- auth_principal
- acting user (if present)
- old_stage/new_stage
- reason
But again: replay should NOT emit audit twice.

Jordan Lee: +1. We should also include `idempotency_replayed=true` flag in response headers? maybe too much.

Priya Natarajan: no need. But add a debug log line when replay occurs.

Ethan Park: so final behaviors:
- same key => same response (200 or 409?), no extra side effects
- different key + same expected version => one wins, other 409
- missing key => 400
- missing expected version => ???

Jordan Lee: missing expected version should be 400 too. Otherwise two parallel promotes from different clients can both succeed sequentially, which is unexpected.

Jada Williams: agree. Promote should be explicit about what you think current stage/version is.

Benji Okafor: ok: require BOTH `Idempotency-Key` AND expected version.

Priya Natarajan: decision: required for all action endpoints.

Priya Natarajan: Benji can you capture in the PR description + update the API docs in the service doc? (Also ping Connor so QA knows what to test.)

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll add test cases:
- retry same key returns same `action_id` + stage
- concurrent promote with different keys -> one 200, one 409
- replay with different payload -> 400

Chloe Martin: I’ll update Console backend to always send both headers. For expected version we’ll use ETag from GET plan.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please add one more test: replay with different auth principal should NOT return cached response (should 409/404). We can decide exact code, but shouldn’t leak.

Jordan Lee: suggest 409 `idempotency_key_conflict` if key exists for different principal within same org/plan. That’s safe.

Benji Okafor: ok.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: thanks all

Priya Natarajan: :thumbsup: ship it, but behind the feature flag until Console is updated.

Benji Okafor: (deploy bot voice) will do.

Chloe Martin: :eyes: I’ll coordinate rollout order with release train so we don’t break existing clients.

title: #architecture: 1739224450
preview:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) telemetry-query svc
- aggregator does shaping + caching + rate limiting + degraded-mode behavior
- slack UX: 3 cards (Deploy / Config / Saturation) + “Open in Console” links

Rafael Mendes: +1 to putting all fan-out behind aggregator. oncall doesn’t care which upstream is sick, they just want “here’s what we know” and “here’s where to click”.

Logan Wright: biggest risk is telemetry load during real incidents. if #incidents has 40 ppl hammering “refresh context” we’ll DDOS ourselves.

Allison Grant: + paging loops. if bot causes extra latency in metrics pipeline, we’ll get more alerts, then more bot usage… spiral.

Ethan Park: question: is aggregator strictly read-only? any chance it triggers state (rollout pauses, routing changes) via interactive actions?

Miles Thornton: for v2, read-only. interactive actions are “expand diff”, “refresh”, “open dashboard”, “copy cli”. no “pause rollout” buttons.

Ravi Menon: read-only is good. we *should* still treat it as production-critical. during partial outages, it must fail predictably.

Devon Price: slack constraint: interactive action needs ACK in 3s. so aggregator has to respond fast or we do the pattern: immediate ack + “loading…” + async update.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve done the async update pattern in other bots. works, but we need idempotency keys so retries don’t post duplicate cards.

Connor O'Brien: deploy metadata path: we can provide last N deploys per service/region w/ rollout phase + canary status. join to PR/commit is possible but not always (hotfixes).

Benji Okafor: config diff: we can return “last 10 config mutations” and/or “diff between current and T-30m snapshot”. we need strong redaction hooks before anything hits Slack.

Marcus Lin: +1. we should assume Slack is not a secure sink. even in private channels. redaction must be server-side and testable.

Kevin Osei: and audit events. every call to config diff or privileged telemetry should emit an audit record (who/what incident/thread).

Naomi Feldman: from compliance side: we’ll want (a) scope-based access, (b) immutable audit trail, (c) clear break-glass rules. also define retention.

Miles Thornton: ok, diving into feedback requested:
1) data flow & boundaries
2) caching + coalescing
3) rate limits / circuit breakers
4) failure modes under partial outage

Ravi Menon: on (1): keep diag-aggregator dumb-ish. it should not encode “business logic” like routing decisions. it should *surface* current policy + pointers.

Mina Haddad: +1. if we embed policy eval in bot, we’ll drift from actual routing engine. better: bot shows pinned model, fallback chain, and “last routing policy change” link.

Jordan Lee: we can expose a “policy snapshot” endpoint that’s already computed by the control plane (safe subset). aggregator should fetch that.

Ethan Park: please make sure “safe subset” is explicit. policy configs can contain tenant annotations we don’t want sprayed in Slack.

Benji Okafor: for config diff, we’re planning allowlist-based rendering. everything else => “[redacted]”. secrets patterns are necessary but not sufficient.

Marcus Lin: exactly. regex-based “secret detection” will miss things and false-positive. allowlist primary, pattern-based secondary.

Allison Grant: on (2) caching: propose two layers?
- L1 in aggregator (in-memory) TTL 30-60s for interactive refresh spam
- L2 shared cache (redis) TTL 2-5m for same incident/thread to smooth bursts

Logan Wright: careful with caching telemetry. folks will treat it as real-time. if it’s 5m stale we need to label “as of” time and a refresh button.

Devon Price: UI-wise, we can show “Updated: 12:31:10Z” and “(cached)” in the card.

Rafael Mendes: + a note in degraded mode: “Live queries timing out; showing last known good context from 2m ago.”

Dylan Brooks: coalescing: yes. key should include {incident_id or thread_ts, service, region, tenant?}. if key is too broad we’ll leak tenant context.

Logan Wright: + on telemetry, avoid tenant in the query unless we need it. default to service/region aggregate for “saturation” card. tenant drill-down should be link out to Console where auth is stronger.

Miles Thornton: noted. current plan is:
- Saturation card = service/region/pool-level only
- Tenant-specific metrics only via deep link w/ RBAC

Aisha Bello: on (3) rate limits: we need budgets per thread and per user action.
- hard cap per thread: e.g. 6 refreshes / 5 min
- global cap in aggregator to protect upstreams
- backoff behavior: after cap hit, show message + links only

Allison Grant: also cap label cardinality. if telemetry query svc is asked for “by model, by tenant, by route, by status_code” we’re dead.

Logan Wright: we should define a strict set of queries the bot is allowed to run. like a fixed query catalog with parameters, not arbitrary promql.

Miles Thornton: yes, query catalog approach. aggregator calls telemetry-query svc with query_id + params (service/region/window). no raw query strings.

Ethan Park: +1. easier to permission and audit.

Kevin Osei: auditing: log (a) caller identity (bot service acct), (b) human user initiating action, (c) query_id, (d) params (redacted), (e) result size, (f) latency, (g) outcome (success/timeout/circuit-open).

Marcus Lin: and for config diff, log “fields returned” count maybe, plus whether any redactions occurred.

Benji Okafor: we can do “redaction_summary: N fields redacted” but keep it in audit logs not Slack.

Ravi Menon: on (4) partial outage behavior: define priorities.
- Must show *something* within ~10s (or slack will feel broken)
- If upstream slow: show cached + deep links
- If cached missing: show “manual commands” snippet (eg `rwctl deploys --service X --region Y`)

Connor O'Brien: for deploys, if deploy metadata svc is down, we can still pull from deploy event stream? maybe last-known state in redis.

Miles Thornton: we can cache deploy context longer (10-15m) since it doesn’t change second-to-second.

Allison Grant: but stamp it. “last deploy event seen at …”

Dylan Brooks: circuit breaker suggestion:
- per-upstream breaker in aggregator
- open when p95 latency > threshold or error rate > threshold
- when open: stop calling upstream for N seconds, serve cache + links

Logan Wright: yes. and telemetry-query svc should also have its own breaker to protect the TSDB.

Rafael Mendes: also, bot should never block incident channel during sev0. if it times out, it should say so plainly (not “unknown error”).

Devon Price: copy for error states:
- “Telemetry query timed out (10s). Showing cached context from 2m ago.”
- “Config diff unavailable: missing scope `config.diff.read`. Escalate via #help (INT ticket) or break-glass if Sev0/Sev1.”

Ethan Park: careful wording on break-glass. should route to the oncall/security process, not encourage random folks.

Naomi Feldman: agree. phrasing should be “Request elevated access via documented Sev0/Sev1 procedure (link).”

Kevin Osei: and the break-glass action itself must generate a high-signal audit event (and ideally page security-oncall if used outside sev0/1).

Miles Thornton: open question: should aggregator run in same cluster as bot or separate?

Logan Wright: separate. if bot gets wedged / restarts, we don’t want it taking cache + rate limiter with it.

Dylan Brooks: + separate deploy lets us roll diag changes without touching slack app bits. but keep it small.

Allison Grant: also consider: aggregator should have low dependency footprint. avoid calling 6 internal services. keep it to 3 + cache.

Priya Natarajan: platform POV: fine to add aggregator, but we need ownership clear. SRE owns? platform owns? shared?

Rafael Mendes: SRE can own runtime/SLIs, but data contracts live w/ respective teams. propose: SRE owns aggregator + bot, platform owns config/policy endpoints, telemetry owns telemetry-query svc, releases owns deploy metadata.

Miles Thornton: action items from review (please correct):
- [Logan] define query catalog + cost budgets; propose default windows + aggregations for saturation
- [Benji + Marcus + Naomi] finalize redaction + safe render allowlist + language for Slack
- [Allison + Aisha] rate limit + circuit breaker thresholds + “degraded mode” card standards
- [Connor] confirm deploy metadata schema + fallback plan when deploy svc unavailable
- [Kevin] audit event spec fields + retention
- [Devon + Dylan] slack async update + idempotency keys pattern for refresh/expand

Logan Wright: looks right. add: we should run a perf canary for telemetry-query latency as part of rollout.

Allison Grant: +1. and alert on “bot context generation latency” separately from core prod latency.

Ethan Park: please document permission scopes early. we’ve had surprises where bot can’t read prod config because role missing.

Miles Thornton: will add “scopes matrix” to the ADR. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: last ask: add tests that assert no forbidden fields can appear in Slack payload (golden tests). treat as security regression.

Benji Okafor: yes. we can add a suite w/ synthetic config that includes fake secrets + PII keys and assert output is redacted.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: ship it (safely).
